lessons.title: Q1: Worker Name Alias
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch `FIRST_NAME` from `worker` table using the alias name as `WORKER_NAME`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT first_name AS WORKER_NAME FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Fetches the first_name column and renames it to WORKER_NAME in the output using the AS alias keyword.

lessons.title: Q2: Upper Case Names
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch `FIRST_NAME` from `worker` table in upper case.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT UPPER(first_name) FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses the UPPER() string function to convert all names to uppercase.

lessons.title: Q3: Unique Departments
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch unique values of `DEPARTMENT` from `worker` table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT DISTINCT department FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses the DISTINCT keyword to remove duplicates and list unique departments.

lessons.title: Q4: Substring Extraction
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print the first three characters of `FIRST_NAME` from `worker` table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT SUBSTRING(first_name, 1, 3) FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Extracts characters from position 1 to 3 of first_name using SUBSTRING().

lessons.title: Q5: Character Positioning
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to find the position of the alphabet ('b') in the first name column 'Amitabh' from `worker` table.
Note: For compatibility, use `INSTR(first_name, 'b')` which is supported in SQLite.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT INSTR(first_name, 'b') FROM worker WHERE first_name = 'Amitabh';
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker WHERE first_name = 'Amitabh';

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: INSTR(string, char) returns the 1-based index position of 'b' in first_name.

lessons.title: Q6: Right Trim Spaces
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print the `FIRST_NAME` from `worker` table after removing white spaces from the right side.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT RTRIM(first_name) FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Removes trailing spaces from first_name using RTRIM().

lessons.title: Q7: Left Trim Spaces
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print the `DEPARTMENT` from `worker` table after removing white spaces from the left side.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT LTRIM(department) FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Removes leading spaces from department using LTRIM().

lessons.title: Q8: Distinct Departments Length
lessons.description: Write an SQL query that fetches the unique values of `DEPARTMENT` from `worker` table and prints its length.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT DISTINCT department, LENGTH(department) FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Combines DISTINCT department with the LENGTH() function to get unique departments and their character length.

lessons.title: Q9: Replace Characters
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print the `FIRST_NAME` from `worker` table after replacing 'a' with 'A'.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT REPLACE(first_name, 'a', 'A') FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Replaces all lowercase 'a' with uppercase 'A' using REPLACE().

lessons.title: Q10: Concatenate Full Name
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to print the `FIRST_NAME` and `LAST_NAME` from `worker` table into a single column `COMPLETE_NAME` separated by a space.
Note: For SQLite support, concatenate strings using the standard double pipe `||` operator (e.g. `first_name || ' ' || last_name`).
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS COMPLETE_NAME FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  AS COMPLETE_NAME FROM worker;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Concatenates first_name and last_name with a space divider using the string concatenation operator.

lessons.title: Q11: Sort by First Name
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print all Worker details from the `worker` table ordered by `FIRST_NAME` Ascending.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY first_name ASC;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Orders records alphabetically by first name.

lessons.title: Q12: Multi-Column Sorting
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print all Worker details from the `worker` table ordered by `FIRST_NAME` Ascending and `DEPARTMENT` Descending.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY first_name ASC, department DESC;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Sorts workers by first name ascending, then by department descending.

lessons.title: Q13: Filter by Set Names
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print details for Workers with the first name as 'Vipul' and 'Satish' from `worker` table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE first_name IN ('Vipul', 'Satish');
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses the IN clause to filter for workers whose first names are either 'Vipul' or 'Satish'.

lessons.title: Q14: Exclude Set Names
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print details of workers excluding first names, 'Vipul' and 'Satish' from `worker` table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE first_name NOT IN ('Vipul', 'Satish');
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Filters out workers with first names 'Vipul' or 'Satish' using NOT IN.

lessons.title: Q15: Filter by Department Prefix
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print details of Workers with `DEPARTMENT` name as 'Admin%'.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE department LIKE 'Admin%';
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses the LIKE wildcard to fetch workers whose department name begins with 'Admin'.

lessons.title: Q16: Contains Wildcard Filter
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print details of the Workers whose `FIRST_NAME` contains 'a'.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE first_name LIKE '%a%';
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses the %a% wildcard to match first names containing 'a' anywhere.

lessons.title: Q17: Ends With Wildcard Filter
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print details of the Workers whose `FIRST_NAME` ends with 'a'.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE first_name LIKE '%a';
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses %a wildcard to match first names ending in 'a'.

lessons.title: Q18: Length & Wildcard Filter
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to print details of the Workers whose `FIRST_NAME` ends with 'h' and contains exactly six alphabets.
Note: In SQL, the underscore `_` matches exactly one character.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE first_name LIKE '_____h';
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Matches names exactly 6 characters long ending with 'h' using 5 underscores and 'h'.

lessons.title: Q19: Salary Range Filter
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print details of the Workers whose `SALARY` lies between 100000 and 500000.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE salary BETWEEN 100000 AND 500000;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Filters records with salary in the specified inclusive range using BETWEEN.

lessons.title: Q20: Datetime Month Filtering
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to print details of the Workers who have joined in Feb'2014.
Note: For SQLite compatibility, use the `strftime` function: `strftime('%Y', joining_date) = '2014' AND strftime('%m', joining_date) = '02'`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE strftime('%Y', joining_date) = '2014' AND strftime('%m', joining_date) = '02';
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Extracts and filters records based on joining year (2014) and month (02/February).

lessons.title: Q21: Department Worker Count
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch the count of employees working in the department 'Admin'.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM worker WHERE department = 'Admin';
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Counts all workers in the Admin department.

lessons.title: Q22: Full Name Range Filter
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch worker full names (first name + space + last name) with salaries >= 50000 and <= 100000.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name FROM worker WHERE salary BETWEEN 50000 AND 100000;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Concatenates names and filters salary between 50,000 and 100,000.

lessons.title: Q23: Department Worker Counts
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch the number of workers for each department in descending order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT department, COUNT(worker_id) AS no_of_worker FROM worker GROUP BY department ORDER BY no_of_worker DESC;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT  FROM worker GROUP BY  ORDER BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Groups workers by department, counts them, and sorts by count descending.

lessons.title: Q24: Join Worker and Title
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print details of the Workers who are also Managers.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT w.* FROM worker AS w INNER JOIN title AS t ON w.worker_id = t.worker_ref_id WHERE t.worker_title = 'Manager';
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT w.* FROM worker AS w INNER JOIN ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Performs an INNER JOIN with title on worker_id, filtering for titles equaling 'Manager'.

lessons.title: Q25: Duplicate Title Count
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch number (more than 1) of same titles in the organization.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT worker_title, COUNT(*) AS count FROM title GROUP BY worker_title HAVING count > 1;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT worker_title,  FROM title GROUP BY  HAVING ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Groups by title and filters for titles with a count greater than 1 using HAVING.

lessons.title: Q26: Retrieve Odd Rows
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to show only odd rows from a table.
Note: In SQLite, check if `worker_id % 2 <> 0`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE worker_id % 2 <> 0;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Selects rows where worker_id has a remainder when divided by 2 (odd rows).

lessons.title: Q27: Retrieve Even Rows
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to show only even rows from a table.
Note: In SQLite, check if `worker_id % 2 = 0`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE worker_id % 2 = 0;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Selects rows where worker_id is divisible by 2 (even rows).

lessons.title: Q28: Clone Table
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to clone a new table structure and data `worker_clone` from `worker` table.
Note: In SQLite, this is accomplished in a single query via: `CREATE TABLE worker_clone AS SELECT * FROM worker;`
lessons.expectedQuery: CREATE TABLE worker_clone AS SELECT * FROM worker;
lessons.defaultCode: CREATE TABLE worker_clone AS ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Creates a duplicate table structure and copies all rows in one operation.

lessons.title: Q29: Intersecting Records
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch intersecting records of `worker` and `worker_clone` tables.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT w.* FROM worker w INNER JOIN worker_clone USING(worker_id);
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT w.* FROM worker w INNER JOIN ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Selects records that are present in both tables using an INNER JOIN.

lessons.title: Q30: Left Join Record Exclusion
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to show records from `worker` table that `worker_clone` table does not have.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT w.* FROM worker w LEFT JOIN worker_clone USING(worker_id) WHERE worker_clone.worker_id IS NULL;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT w.* FROM worker w LEFT JOIN worker_clone USING(worker_id) WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Performs a LEFT JOIN and filters for rows where the matching key in the right table is NULL.

lessons.title: Q31: Show Current Date & Time
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to show the current date and time.
Note: For SQLite, return the results as `current_date` and `current_time` columns using: `SELECT date('now') AS current_date, time('now') AS current_time;`
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT date('now') AS current_date, time('now') AS current_time;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT date('now') AS current_date, ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Fetches current system UTC date and time in SQLite.

lessons.title: Q32: Top N Earners
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to show the top 5 records of `worker` table ordered by descending salary.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 5;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY  LIMIT ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Sorts salaries in descending order and restricts the output to 5 rows.

lessons.title: Q33: Determine Nth Salary
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to determine the 5th highest salary from a table.
Note: Use `LIMIT offset, row_count` (which translates to `LIMIT 4, 1` to skip the first 4 and get 1).
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 4, 1;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY  LIMIT ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Sorts by salary descending and uses offsets in LIMIT to target the 5th row.

lessons.title: Q34: Nth Salary without LIMIT
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to determine the 5th highest salary without using the `LIMIT` keyword.
Note: Use a correlated subquery counting distinct salaries higher than or equal to the target.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT salary FROM worker w1 WHERE 4 = (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT w2.salary) FROM worker w2 WHERE w2.salary >= w1.salary);
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT salary FROM worker w1 WHERE 4 = (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT w2.salary) FROM worker w2 WHERE );

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Finds the salary where exactly 4 distinct salaries are larger than or equal to it.

lessons.title: Q35: Employees with Same Salary
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch the list of employees with the same salary.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT w1.* FROM worker w1, worker w2 WHERE w1.salary = w2.salary AND w1.worker_id != w2.worker_id;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT w1.* FROM worker w1, worker w2 WHERE w1.salary = w2.salary AND ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Self-joins the worker table to match employees having matching salaries but unique IDs.

lessons.title: Q36: Second Highest Salary
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to show the second highest salary from a table using a subquery.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM worker WHERE salary NOT IN (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM worker);
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM worker WHERE salary NOT IN (SELECT  FROM worker);

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Finds the maximum salary excluding the absolute maximum salary.

lessons.title: Q37: Duplicate Rows in Result
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to show one row twice in results from a table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker UNION ALL SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY worker_id;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker UNION ALL  ORDER BY worker_id;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Performs a UNION ALL of the table with itself to duplicate all rows.

lessons.title: Q38: Workers Without Bonus
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to list `worker_id` who does not get a bonus.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT worker_id FROM worker WHERE worker_id NOT IN (SELECT worker_ref_id FROM bonus);
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT worker_id FROM worker WHERE worker_id NOT IN (SELECT  FROM bonus);

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Finds worker_ids that do not appear in the bonus table.

lessons.title: Q39: Fetch First Half Records
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch the first 50% records from a table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE worker_id <= (SELECT COUNT(worker_id)/2 FROM worker);
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE worker_id <= (SELECT );

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Selects rows up to the index matching half of the total row count.

lessons.title: Q40: Departments with Under 4 People
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch the departments that have less than 4 people in it.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT department, COUNT(department) AS depCount FROM worker GROUP BY department HAVING depCount < 4;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT department, COUNT(department) AS depCount FROM worker GROUP BY department HAVING ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Groups by department and filters departments where aggregate count is less than 4.

lessons.title: Q41: All Departments count
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to show all departments along with the number of people in there.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT department, COUNT(department) AS depCount FROM worker GROUP BY department;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT department,  FROM worker GROUP BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Groups by department and counts total workers inside each department.

lessons.title: Q42: Show Last Record
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to show the last record from `worker` table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE worker_id = (SELECT MAX(worker_id) FROM worker);
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE worker_id = (SELECT  FROM worker);

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Finds the row corresponding to the maximum worker_id.

lessons.title: Q43: Show First Record
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch the first row of a table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE worker_id = (SELECT MIN(worker_id) FROM worker);
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM worker WHERE worker_id = (SELECT  FROM worker);

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Finds the row corresponding to the minimum worker_id.

lessons.title: Q44: Fetch Last Five Records
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to fetch the last five records from a table.
Note: For SQLite, select the last 5 records using a subquery and sort them ascending: `SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY worker_id DESC LIMIT 5) ORDER BY worker_id ASC;`
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY worker_id DESC LIMIT 5) ORDER BY worker_id ASC;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM worker ORDER BY  LIMIT 5) ORDER BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Pulls the last 5 rows by sorting in descending order, then sorts them back ascending.

lessons.title: Q45: Highest Salary in Department
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to print the name of employees having the highest salary in each department.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT w.department, w.first_name, w.salary FROM (SELECT MAX(salary) AS maxsal, department FROM worker GROUP BY department) temp INNER JOIN worker w ON temp.department = w.department AND temp.maxsal = w.salary;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT w.department, w.first_name, w.salary FROM (SELECT  FROM worker GROUP BY ) temp INNER JOIN worker w ON ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Finds the maximum salary grouped by department, then joins with worker to identify the employee names.

lessons.title: Q46: Three Max Salaries
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch three max salaries from a table using a correlated subquery.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM worker w1 WHERE 3 >= (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT salary) FROM worker w2 WHERE w1.salary <= w2.salary) ORDER BY w1.salary DESC;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM worker w1 WHERE 3 >= (SELECT  FROM worker w2 WHERE ) ORDER BY w1.salary DESC;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Retrieves the top 3 distinct salaries by counting how many salaries are larger than or equal to the target.

lessons.title: Q47: Three Min Salaries
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch three min salaries from a table using a correlated subquery.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM worker w1 WHERE 3 >= (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT salary) FROM worker w2 WHERE w1.salary >= w2.salary) ORDER BY w1.salary ASC;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM worker w1 WHERE 3 >= (SELECT  FROM worker w2 WHERE ) ORDER BY w1.salary ASC;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Retrieves the lowest 3 distinct salaries by counting how many salaries are smaller than or equal to the target.

lessons.title: Q48: Nth Max Salaries
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch top 5 (nth where n=5) max salaries from a table.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM worker w1 WHERE 5 >= (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT salary) FROM worker w2 WHERE w1.salary <= w2.salary) ORDER BY w1.salary DESC;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM worker w1 WHERE 5 >= (SELECT  FROM worker w2 WHERE ) ORDER BY w1.salary DESC;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Retrieves the top 5 distinct salaries using a correlated subquery.

lessons.title: Q49: Total Salary by Department
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch departments along with the total salaries paid for each of them.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT department, SUM(salary) AS depSal FROM worker GROUP BY department ORDER BY depSal DESC;
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT department,  FROM worker GROUP BY  ORDER BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Aggregates total salary by department and sorts them from highest to lowest.

lessons.title: Q50: Names of Highest Earners
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to fetch the names of workers who earn the highest salary.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT first_name, salary FROM worker WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM worker);
lessons.defaultCode: SELECT first_name, salary FROM worker WHERE salary = (SELECT  FROM worker);

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Filters workers whose salary matches the maximum salary in the table.

title: Chapter 27: SQL Exercise Set B - Basic Select (Questions 1–20)

lessons.title: Revising the Select Query I
lessons.description:
Query all columns for all American cities in the **CITY** table with populations larger than 100,000. The CountryCode for America is `USA`.

**Hint:** Filter by `countrycode` and `population` using the `AND` logical operator.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM CITY WHERE countrycode = 'USA' AND population > 100000;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Query American cities population > 100,000
SELECT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Selects all columns where countrycode matches 'USA' and population is greater than 100,000.

lessons.title: Revising the Select Query II
lessons.description:
Query the **NAME** field for all American cities in the **CITY** table with populations larger than 120,000. The CountryCode for America is `USA`.

**Hint:** Project only the `name` column instead of `*`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT name FROM CITY WHERE countrycode = 'USA' AND population > 120000;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Query American city names population > 120,000
SELECT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Selects only the name column where countrycode matches 'USA' and population is greater than 120,000.

lessons.title: Select All
lessons.description:
Query all columns (attributes) for every row in the **CITY** table.

**Hint:** Do not write any `WHERE` clause filter.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM CITY;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Select all records
SELECT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Extracts the complete table attributes by omitting any filtering conditions.

lessons.title: Select By ID
lessons.description:
Query all columns for a city in **CITY** with the ID `1661`.

**Hint:** Use `WHERE id = 1661`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM CITY WHERE id = 1661;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Select by ID
SELECT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Filters for a unique record using the primary key ID constraint.

lessons.title: Japanese Cities' Attributes
lessons.description:
Query all attributes of every Japanese city in the **CITY** table. The CountryCode for Japan is `JPN`.

**Hint:** Filter by countrycode equaling 'JPN'.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT * FROM CITY WHERE countrycode = 'JPN';
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Japanese cities attributes
SELECT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Queries all columns filtering by Japan's country code.

lessons.title: Japanese Cities' Names
lessons.description:
Query the names of all the Japanese cities in the **CITY** table. The CountryCode for Japan is `JPN`.

**Hint:** Project only the `name` column.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT name FROM CITY WHERE countrycode = 'JPN';
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Japanese cities names
SELECT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Selects name attribute for records matching Japanese country code.

lessons.title: Weather Observation Station 1
lessons.description:
Query a list of **CITY** and **STATE** from the **STATION** table.

**Hint:** Select two column names separated by a comma.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT city, state FROM STATION;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Query CITY and STATE
SELECT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Retrieves the geographic details of cities and their states.

lessons.title: Weather Observation Station 3
lessons.description:
Query a list of **CITY** names from **STATION** for stations with an even **ID** number. Exclude duplicates from your answer.

**Hint:** Combine `DISTINCT` and the modulo operator `%` (`id % 2 = 0`).
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT DISTINCT city FROM STATION WHERE id % 2 = 0;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Distinct cities with even IDs
SELECT DISTINCT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Filters for records with even keys and removes repeating items using DISTINCT.

lessons.title: Weather Observation Station 4
lessons.description:
Find the difference between the total number of **CITY** entries in the table and the number of distinct **CITY** entries in the table.

**Hint:** Compute the mathematical difference: `COUNT(city) - COUNT(DISTINCT city)`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT COUNT(city) - COUNT(DISTINCT city) FROM STATION;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Difference in total and distinct counts
SELECT

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Subtracts the unique count of cities from the overall rows count to identify duplicates density.

lessons.title: Weather Observation Station 5
lessons.description:
Query the two cities in **STATION** with the shortest and longest **CITY** names, as well as their respective lengths (i.e.: number of characters in the name). If there is more than one smallest or largest city, choose the one that comes first alphabetically.

**Hint:** Combine short and long query rows using `UNION ALL` with individual ordered `LIMIT 1` subqueries.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT city, LENGTH(city) FROM (SELECT city FROM STATION ORDER BY LENGTH(city) ASC, city ASC LIMIT 1) UNION ALL SELECT city, LENGTH(city) FROM (SELECT city FROM STATION ORDER BY LENGTH(city) DESC, city ASC LIMIT 1);

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Sorts city names by character lengths and alphabetically to slice out the extreme bounds using UNION.

title: Chapter 28: SQL Exercise Set B - Advanced Select (Questions 1–5)

lessons.title: Type of Triangle
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to identify the type of each record in the **TRIANGLES** table using its three side lengths. Output one of: `Equilateral`, `Isosceles`, `Scalene`, or `Not A Triangle`.

**Hint:** A triangle is invalid if `a + b <= c` or `b + c <= a` or `a + c <= b`. Use a `CASE WHEN` statement.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN a + b <= c OR b + c <= a OR a + c <= b THEN 'Not A Triangle' WHEN a = b AND b = c THEN 'Equilateral' WHEN a = b OR b = c OR a = c THEN 'Isosceles' ELSE 'Scalene' END FROM TRIANGLES;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Identify triangle types
SELECT CASE 
  WHEN 
END
FROM TRIANGLES;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses conditional CASE checking triangle validity first, then side equality constraints.

lessons.title: The PADS
lessons.description:
Query an alphabetically ordered list of all names in **OCCUPATIONS**, immediately followed by the first letter of each occupation as a parenthetical (e.g.: `Aniket(D)`). Then, query the number of occurrences of each occupation. Sort the occurrences in ascending order, formatted as: `There are a total of [occupation_count] [occupation]s.`

**Hint:** In SQLite, combine lists using `UNION ALL` wrapping sorting structures.

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Combines a name listing query with an aggregated count query, sorting distinct groups using subquery sorting tags.

lessons.title: Occupations
lessons.description:
Pivot the **OCCUPATIONS** table so that each Name is sorted alphabetically and displayed under its corresponding Occupation. The column headers should be `Doctor`, `Professor`, `Singer`, and `Actor` in order.

**Hint:** Use the window function `ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY occupation ORDER BY name)` to assign sequential row indices before grouping.
lessons.expectedQuery:
WITH Ranked AS (
  SELECT name, occupation,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY occupation ORDER BY name) as rn
  FROM OCCUPATIONS
)
SELECT
  MAX(CASE WHEN occupation = 'Doctor' THEN name END) AS Doctor,
  MAX(CASE WHEN occupation = 'Professor' THEN name END) AS Professor,
  MAX(CASE WHEN occupation = 'Singer' THEN name END) AS Singer,
  MAX(CASE WHEN occupation = 'Actor' THEN name END) AS Actor
FROM Ranked
GROUP BY rn
ORDER BY rn;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Pivot occupations table
WITH Ranked AS (
  SELECT name, occupation,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY occupation ORDER BY name) as rn
  FROM OCCUPATIONS
)
SELECT
  MAX(CASE WHEN occupation = 'Doctor' THEN name END) AS Doctor
  -- Add other columns here...
FROM Ranked
GROUP BY rn
ORDER BY rn;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Pivots dynamic names into static columns by grouping row-number partitioned indexes for each occupation category.

lessons.title: Binary Tree Nodes
lessons.description:
Write a query to find the node type of *Binary Tree* in **BST** table ordered by the value of the node. Output `Root`, `Inner`, or `Leaf`.

**Hint:** A node is 'Root' if its parent `P` is NULL, 'Inner' if its ID `N` exists in the parent column, and 'Leaf' otherwise.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT n, CASE WHEN p IS NULL THEN 'Root' WHEN n IN (SELECT DISTINCT p FROM BST WHERE p IS NOT NULL) THEN 'Inner' ELSE 'Leaf' END FROM BST ORDER BY n;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Classify tree nodes
SELECT n, CASE 
  WHEN p IS NULL THEN 'Root'
  -- Complete classification logic
END 
FROM BST 
ORDER BY n;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Identifies hierarchy nodes using root checking and nested subquery lookups to find inner parents.

lessons.title: New Companies
lessons.description:
For each company, query the company_code, founder name, and count of distinct lead managers, senior managers, managers, and employees. Sort by company_code ascending.

**Hint:** Join the hierarchy tables on company_code and query `COUNT(DISTINCT ...)` groups.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT c.company_code, c.founder,
       COUNT(DISTINCT lm.lead_manager_code),
       COUNT(DISTINCT sm.senior_manager_code),
       COUNT(DISTINCT m.manager_code),
       COUNT(DISTINCT e.employee_id)
FROM Company c
LEFT JOIN Lead_Manager lm ON c.company_code = lm.company_code
LEFT JOIN Senior_Manager sm ON c.company_code = sm.company_code
LEFT JOIN Manager m ON c.company_code = m.company_code
LEFT JOIN Employee e ON c.company_code = e.company_code
GROUP BY c.company_code, c.founder
ORDER BY c.company_code ASC;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Join and aggregate company hierarchies
SELECT c.company_code, c.founder
FROM Company c
-- Join other tables here...
GROUP BY c.company_code, c.founder
ORDER BY c.company_code ASC;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Consolidates organizational totals by left-joining company nodes and counting unique IDs.

lessons.title: Weather Observation Station 18
lessons.description:
Consider $P_1(a, c)$ and $P_2(b, d)$ to be two points on a 2D plane where $a$ is the minimum value of Northern Latitude, $b$ is the maximum value of Northern Latitude, $c$ is the minimum value of Western Longitude, and $d$ is the maximum value of Western Longitude. Query the *Manhattan Distance* between points $P_1$ and $P_2$, rounded to `4` decimal places.

**Hint:** Manhattan distance formula is: `|a - b| + |c - d|` which maps to `ABS(MIN(lat_n) - MAX(lat_n)) + ABS(MIN(long_w) - MAX(long_w))`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT ROUND(ABS(MIN(lat_n) - MAX(lat_n)) + ABS(MIN(long_w) - MAX(long_w)), 4) FROM STATION;

lessons.title: Weather Observation Station 19

Query the *Euclidean Distance* between points $P_1$ and $P_2$, rounded to `4` decimal places.

**Hint:** Euclidean distance formula is: `sqrt((a - b)^2 + (c - d)^2)`. Use `SQRT` and `POWER`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT ROUND(SQRT(POWER(MIN(lat_n) - MAX(lat_n), 2) + POWER(MIN(long_w) - MAX(long_w), 2)), 4) FROM STATION;

lessons.title: Weather Observation Station 20

Query the *Median* of the Northern Latitudes (**LAT_N**) from **STATION** and round your answer to `4` decimal places.

**Hint:** Calculate the median index using window function `ROW_NUMBER() OVER(ORDER BY lat_n)` and filtering for the middle row.
lessons.expectedQuery:
WITH Sorted AS (
  SELECT lat_n,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER(ORDER BY lat_n) as rn,
         COUNT(*) OVER() as cnt
  FROM STATION
)
SELECT ROUND(AVG(lat_n), 4) FROM Sorted
WHERE rn BETWEEN cnt/2.0 AND cnt/2.0 + 1;

FROM Sorted
WHERE rn BETWEEN ;

lessons.title: The Report
lessons.description:
Generate a report containing three columns: Name, Grade and Mark. Names of students who got grades lower than 8 should be replaced with `NULL`. Sort by Grade descending, then Name ascending, then Marks ascending.

**Hint:** Join students and grades where marks are between `min_mark` and `max_mark`.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN g.grade >= 8 THEN s.name ELSE NULL END AS name_or_null, g.grade, s.marks
FROM STUDENTS s
JOIN Grades g ON s.marks BETWEEN g.min_mark AND g.max_mark
ORDER BY g.grade DESC,
         CASE WHEN g.grade >= 8 THEN s.name END ASC,
         CASE WHEN g.grade < 8 THEN s.marks END ASC;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Student grades report
SELECT CASE WHEN g.grade >= 8 THEN s.name ELSE NULL END, g.grade, s.marks
FROM STUDENTS s
JOIN Grades g ON s.marks BETWEEN 
ORDER BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Matches student marks to boundary grades and uses conditional aliases to protect low scoring student names.

lessons.title: Top Competitors
lessons.description:
Write a query to print the hacker_id and name of hackers who achieved full scores for more than one challenge. Sort by counts descending, then hacker_id ascending.

**Hint:** Join Hackers, Submissions, Challenges, and Difficulty tables where `Submissions.score = Difficulty.score`.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT h.hacker_id, h.name
FROM Hackers h
JOIN Submissions s ON h.hacker_id = s.hacker_id
JOIN Challenges c ON s.challenge_id = c.challenge_id
JOIN Difficulty d ON c.difficulty_level = d.difficulty_level
WHERE s.score = d.score
GROUP BY h.hacker_id, h.name
HAVING COUNT(s.challenge_id) > 1
ORDER BY COUNT(s.challenge_id) DESC, h.hacker_id ASC;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Find hackers with multiple full scores
SELECT h.hacker_id, h.name
FROM Hackers h
JOIN Submissions s ON h.hacker_id = s.hacker_id
-- Join challenges and difficulty
WHERE s.score = d.score
GROUP BY h.hacker_id, h.name
HAVING COUNT(s.challenge_id) > 1
ORDER BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Finds competitive submissions where the user's score matches maximum difficulty points.

lessons.title: Ollivander's Inventory
lessons.description:
Hermione wants to buy only non-evil wands with the minimum number of gold galleons needed to buy each unique wand of high power and age. Output: id, age, coins_needed, and power. Sort by power desc, then age desc.

**Hint:** Use a correlated subquery checking `coins_needed` matches the minimum coins for wands with matching power and code.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT w.id, wp.age, w.coins_needed, w.power
FROM Wands w
JOIN Wands_Property wp ON w.code = wp.code
WHERE wp.is_evil = 0 AND w.coins_needed = (
  SELECT MIN(w2.coins_needed)
  FROM Wands w2
  JOIN Wands_Property wp2 ON w2.code = wp2.code
  WHERE w2.power = w.power AND wp2.age = wp.age
)
ORDER BY w.power DESC, wp.age DESC;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Cheapest high-power non-evil wands
SELECT w.id, wp.age, w.coins_needed, w.power
FROM Wands w
JOIN Wands_Property wp ON w.code = wp.code
WHERE wp.is_evil = 0 AND w.coins_needed = (
  -- Subquery minimum coins
)
ORDER BY w.power DESC, wp.age DESC;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses a correlated lookup to filter out wands with higher costs for each power-age pairing.

lessons.title: Challenges
lessons.description:
Query the hacker_id, name, and total challenges created. Sort by count desc, then hacker_id. If more than one hacker created the same number of challenges, and that number is less than the max number of challenges, exclude those hackers.

**Hint:** Use CTEs to calculate challenge counts per hacker, count their frequencies, and filter.
lessons.expectedQuery:
WITH ChallengeCounts AS (
  SELECT hacker_id, COUNT(*) as cnt
  FROM Challenges
  GROUP BY hacker_id
),
GroupedCounts AS (
  SELECT cnt, COUNT(*) as cnt_of_cnt
  FROM ChallengeCounts
  GROUP BY cnt
)
SELECT h.hacker_id, h.name, cc.cnt
FROM Hackers h
JOIN ChallengeCounts cc ON h.hacker_id = cc.hacker_id
WHERE cc.cnt = (SELECT MAX(cnt) FROM ChallengeCounts)
   OR cc.cnt IN (SELECT cnt FROM GroupedCounts WHERE cnt_of_cnt = 1)
ORDER BY cc.cnt DESC, h.hacker_id ASC;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- CTE calculation of challenge statistics
WITH ChallengeCounts AS (
  SELECT hacker_id, COUNT(*) as cnt
  FROM Challenges
  GROUP BY hacker_id
)
SELECT h.hacker_id, h.name, cc.cnt
FROM Hackers h
JOIN ChallengeCounts cc ON h.hacker_id = cc.hacker_id
WHERE -- Filter constraints
ORDER BY cc.cnt DESC, h.hacker_id ASC;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Calculates challenge distributions and filters out duplicate count rows unless they are the absolute maximum.

lessons.title: Contest Leaderboard
lessons.description:
Query the hacker_id, name, and total score. The total score is the sum of maximum scores for all challenges. Sort by total score desc, then hacker_id. Exclude hackers with a score of 0.

**Hint:** Create a subquery that gets `MAX(score)` grouped by challenge_id and hacker_id.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT h.hacker_id, h.name, SUM(max_score) AS total_score
FROM Hackers h
JOIN (
  SELECT hacker_id, MAX(score) AS max_score
  FROM Submissions
  GROUP BY hacker_id, challenge_id
) s ON h.hacker_id = s.hacker_id
GROUP BY h.hacker_id, h.name
HAVING total_score > 0
ORDER BY total_score DESC, h.hacker_id ASC;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Aggregate maximum scores sum

FROM Hackers h
JOIN (
  -- Max score grouped by challenge
) s ON 
GROUP BY h.hacker_id, h.name
HAVING total_score > 0
ORDER BY ;

title: Chapter 31: SQL Exercise Set B - Advanced Joins (Questions 1–2)

lessons.title: SQL Project Planning
lessons.description:
Query the start date and end date of projects, listed by the number of days it takes to complete the project in ascending order. If there is more than one project that has the same number of completion days, then order by start date.

**Hint:** A start_date begins a project if it's not in the end_date column. An end_date closes it if it's not in the start_date column. Join them on `start_date < end_date` grouping by `start_date` to find `MIN(end_date)`.

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Filters project bounds using sets inclusions, counting completed interval duration using julian days subtraction.

lessons.title: Placements
lessons.description:
Write a query to output the names of those students whose best friends got offered a higher salary than them. Names must be ordered by the salary amount offered to the best friends.

**Hint:** Join Students, Friends, and Packages (twice) to get the student's salary and their best friend's salary.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT s.name
FROM Students s
JOIN Friends f ON s.id = f.id
JOIN Packages p1 ON s.id = p1.id
JOIN Packages p2 ON f.friend_id = p2.id
WHERE p2.salary > p1.salary
ORDER BY p2.salary ASC;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Friend salaries comparison
SELECT s.name
FROM Students s
JOIN Friends f ON s.id = f.id
-- Join packages for self and friend

ORDER BY ;

lessons.explanation: Joins salary packages for students and their friends, filtering for records where friend earnings exceed self earnings.
